subject: Science
overall_learning_objectives: Identify the basic structure and function of a neuron
Understand the concept of reflex action and the reflex arc
Recognize the main parts of the human brain and their simple functions
Identify the major types of plant movements (tropisms)
Identify the main endocrine glands and the hormones they produce
Understand the importance of coordination in living organisms

lesson_plan.title: The Nervous System and Neurons
topics_covered: Structure of a Neuron (Dendrite, Cell body, Axon)
teaching_strategy: Analogy: Telephone wires to explain electrical impulses
Drawing a large labeled diagram of a neuron on the board
Story: How information travels from a touch to the brain
student_activities: Draw and color a neuron in the notebook
Label the parts where a signal is received and where it leaves
assessment: Oral quiz: What is the long part of a neuron called?
Identifying the gap between two neurons (Synapse)

lesson_plan.title: Reflex Actions
teaching_strategy: Demonstration: The 'Knee-jerk' reflex or blinking when an object comes close
Flowchart: Stimulus → Receptor → Spinal Cord → Effector → Response
Discussion: Why doesn't the brain handle reflex actions immediately?
student_activities: Role-play the path of a reflex arc (students as sensory and motor nerves)
List 3 examples of reflex actions from daily life
assessment: Matching: Stimulus to its specific Receptor

lesson_plan.title: The Human Brain
topics_covered: Protection of the Brain (Cranium and Fluid)
teaching_strategy: Showing a 3D model or image of the brain
Function-to-Action mapping (e.g., Cerebellum = Balance)
Discussion: How do we walk in a straight line?
student_activities: Color different parts of a pre-printed brain diagram
Try a balance test (standing on one leg) to understand the Cerebellum
assessment: Short answer: Which part of the brain controls heartbeat?
Naming the 'Thinking part' of the brain

lesson_plan.title: Coordination in Plants - Part 1
topics_covered: Electrical-chemical means of communication in plants
teaching_strategy: Video clip: The 'Touch-me-not' plant folding its leaves
Comparison: Human movement vs. Plant movement
Explaining how water movement causes leaf folding
student_activities: Observe a real Mimosa plant (if available) and record time taken to reopen
Write a short paragraph on how plants 'feel' touch

lesson_plan.title: Coordination in Plants - Tropisms
teaching_strategy: Showing a plant growing toward a window
Drawing diagrams of roots growing down and shoots growing up
Mnemonic: **Photo** for Light, **Geo** for Earth
student_activities: Draw a diagram showing a plant bending toward light
assessment: Quick-fire round: Which part shows positive geotropism?

lesson_plan.title: Hormones in Animals
topics_covered: Endocrine glands: Pituitary, Thyroid, Adrenal, Pancreas
Functions of Insulin, Adrenaline, and Thyroxine
teaching_strategy: Story: The 'Fight or Flight' response (Adrenaline)
Table construction: Gland → Hormone → Function
Discussing the importance of Iodine and the Thyroid
student_activities: Create a 'Hormone ID Card' for Adrenaline and Insulin
Discuss: What happens when we are scared?
assessment: Identifying the hormone used for sugar control

lesson_plan.title: Revision and Summary Assessment
topics_covered: Chapter recap: Brain, Plants, and Hormones
teaching_strategy: Interactive mind-map on the board
Solving NCERT 'In-text' and 'Exercise' questions
Clarifying final doubts
student_activities: Solve a 15-mark MCQ and short answer test
Complete a summary chart of the whole chapter

teacher_tip: For the brain section, use mnemonics like 'Cere-balance' for Cerebellum. At this level, don't worry about complex anatomical terms; focus on the 'location and job' of each part.
